knowledge.what_it_is: The internet is a global network of interconnected computers and servers that communicate using standardized protocols, enabling data exchange between devices worldwide.
what_you_will_learn: TCP/IP model — Transmission Control Protocol/Internet Protocol stack: understanding how data packets are routed across networks
HTTP request-response cycle — client-server interaction: sending requests, receiving responses, and handling status codes (200-500)
DNS resolution — domain name system: mapping human-readable domain names to IP addresses for network communication
IP addressing — IPv4 vs. IPv6: understanding the differences between version 4 and version 6 internet protocol addresses
Network protocols — TCP, UDP, ICMP: learning about transport-layer protocols for reliable data transfer and error handling
Packet switching — how data is fragmented and routed across networks using routers and switches
Internet service providers (ISPs) — understanding the role of ISPs in connecting users to the global network

knowledge.what_it_is: HTTP (Hypertext Transfer Protocol) is a request-response protocol used for data communication between client and server over the internet, enabling web browsers to fetch resources from servers. It's a crucial protocol for frontend developers to understand, as they often work with HTTP requests and responses in their daily tasks.
what_you_will_learn: HTTP Methods — Understanding GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, PATCH, HEAD, OPTIONS, CONNECT, TRACE, and their usage scenarios
Request Headers — Learning about Accept, Accept-Language, Accept-Encoding, User-Agent, Referer, Host, and other common headers used in HTTP requests
Response Status Codes — Familiarizing with 1xx (Informational), 2xx (Successful), 3xx (Redirection), 4xx (Client Error), and 5xx (Server Error) status codes
HTTP Caching — Understanding Cache-Control, Expires, ETag, Last-Modified headers for efficient caching of resources
Content Negotiation — Learning about Accept-Language, Content-Type, and other headers used to negotiate resource representation
HTTP Authentication — Familiarizing with Basic Auth, Digest Auth, and other authentication mechanisms used in HTTP requests

knowledge.what_it_is: A domain name is a human-readable address that maps to an IP address, used to identify and access websites on the internet. In frontend development, domain names are crucial for building web applications that interact with backends or APIs.
what_you_will_learn: Domain Name System (DNS) — resolving domain names to IP addresses using recursive DNS queries
Subdomains — creating nested domains within a parent domain using dot notation (.example.com)
Top-Level Domains (TLDs) — generic extensions like .com, .org, or country-code TLDs like .us, .uk
Domain registration — acquiring a domain name through registrars like GoDaddy or Namecheap
DNS propagation — understanding the time it takes for DNS changes to propagate globally
Canonicalization — ensuring consistent URL formats across different domains and subdomains
Relative URLs — generating URLs relative to the current page's location using JavaScript
Absolute URLs — constructing full URLs with domain, path, query parameters, and fragment

knowledge.what_it_is: Hosting refers to the process of making a website or web application accessible via the internet by storing its files on a remote server, which can be accessed through a unique domain name or IP address. In the context of frontend development, hosting involves deploying and serving static assets such as HTML, CSS, and JavaScript files.
what_you_will_learn: Static website generators — tools like Jekyll, Hugo, and Middleman that automate building and deployment of static websites
Serverless hosting — platforms like Netlify, Vercel, and AWS Lambda that allow deploying serverless functions and APIs
Containerization — using Docker to package and deploy applications in containers for consistent and portable environments
Cloud providers — understanding the differences between AWS, Google Cloud, Microsoft Azure, and IBM Cloud for hosting and deployment
CDN (Content Delivery Network) — optimizing website performance by caching static assets at edge locations worldwide
Version control integration — using Git to manage code changes and automate deployments through tools like GitHub Actions or CircleCI

knowledge.what_it_is: DNS (Domain Name System) is a distributed database that translates human-readable domain names into IP addresses, enabling communication between devices on the internet. As a frontend developer, understanding DNS is crucial for building scalable and reliable web applications.
what_you_will_learn: DNS resolution — how browsers resolve domain names to IP addresses using recursive resolvers like Google Public DNS or Cloudflare DNS
DNS caching — how browsers cache DNS resolutions for faster subsequent lookups, including TTL (time-to-live) management
DNS over HTTPS (DoH) — a protocol that encrypts DNS queries and responses between the client and resolver, improving security and privacy
DNSSEC (Domain Name System Security Extensions) — a suite of protocols that adds cryptographic signatures to DNS data, preventing tampering and spoofing attacks
CDN (Content Delivery Network) integration with DNS — how CDNs use DNS to distribute content across multiple edge locations and reduce latency
DNS-based load balancing — using DNS to distribute traffic across multiple servers or applications based on factors like geographic location or server capacity

knowledge.what_it_is: A web browser is an application that renders HTML, CSS, and JavaScript files on a user's device, allowing them to interact with web pages. Popular browsers include Google Chrome, Mozilla Firefox, Safari, and Microsoft Edge.
what_you_will_learn: Rendering pipeline — the sequence of steps a browser takes to render a webpage: parsing, tree construction, layout, painting
DOM (Document Object Model) — the browser's internal representation of the webpage as a tree-like data structure
CSSOM (CSS Object Model) — the browser's internal representation of the webpage's styles as a tree-like data structure
Layout and paint engines — the algorithms used to position and render elements on the page, including inline layout, block layout, and flexbox
Event loop and event handling — how browsers manage user interactions, such as clicks and keyboard input, and trigger corresponding events in JavaScript code
Security features — how browsers protect users from malicious scripts and websites, including same-origin policy, CORS, and Content Security Policy (CSP)
Storage mechanisms — how browsers store data locally on the client-side, including cookies, local storage, session storage, and IndexedDB
Performance optimization techniques — how to improve browser performance, including code splitting, lazy loading, and caching

knowledge.what_it_is: HTML (Hypertext Markup Language) is the standard markup language used to create web pages, defining structure and content of a webpage. It is not a programming language but rather a declarative way to add meaning to plain text.
what_you_will_learn: Tags — HTML elements wrapped in angle brackets (<tag>content</tag>)
Elements — basic building blocks of an HTML document, such as headings, paragraphs, and links

Attributes — additional information about an element, like its color or size (e.g.,

Hello

)
Semantic HTML — using HTML elements that convey meaning to assistive technologies and search engines (e.g., <header>, <nav>, <main>)
HTML Structure — understanding the basic structure of an HTML document, including doctype declaration, html element, head and body sections

knowledge.what_it_is: Semantic HTML is the practice of writing HTML that clearly and accurately conveys its structure and meaning to both users and search engines, improving accessibility and SEO. It involves using semantic elements like <header>, <nav>, <main>, <section>, and <footer> to define the purpose of each part of a web page.
what_you_will_learn: Semantic elements — using HTML5 elements like <header>, <nav>, <main>, <section>, and <footer> to define the purpose of each part of a web page
ARIA attributes — adding accessibility features with ARIA roles, states, and properties for screen readers and other assistive technologies
Semantic structure — organizing content using semantic elements, headings, and subheadings to create a clear hierarchical structure
Microformats — using microformats like hCard, hCalendar, and hReview to add machine-readable data to HTML documents
Schema.org markup — adding schema.org metadata to improve search engine understanding of web page content and context
HTML5 semantic elements for forms — using <label>, <fieldset>, and <legend> to make forms more accessible and user-friendly

knowledge.what_it_is: Search Engine Optimization (SEO) refers to the practice of improving the visibility and ranking of web pages in search engine results, particularly Google, through various techniques that make content more accessible, crawlable, and relevant to users. As a frontend developer, understanding SEO basics is crucial for building user-friendly and search-engine-optimized websites.
what_you_will_learn: Keyword research — identifying relevant keywords and phrases for on-page optimization using tools like Google Keyword Planner, Ahrefs, or SEMrush
Meta tags — writing descriptive title tags, meta descriptions, and header tags (H1-H6) to provide search engines with essential information about page content
Header tags — structuring content with H1-H6 headings to improve readability and help search engines understand hierarchy
Image optimization — compressing images using tools like ImageOptim or ShortPixel, adding alt text, and specifying image dimensions for faster loading times
Mobile-friendliness — ensuring websites are responsive and provide a good user experience on various devices and screen sizes
Page speed optimization — improving website performance by minifying CSS and JavaScript files, leveraging browser caching, and using content delivery networks (CDNs)
Internal linking — creating clear navigation and linking to relevant pages within the website to improve user experience and search engine crawlability
XML sitemap submission — submitting a sitemap to Google Search Console to help search engines understand website structure and content

knowledge.what_it_is: Forms and validations are essential components of frontend development, enabling users to interact with web applications by submitting data through forms, while ensuring that the submitted data meets specific criteria.
what_you_will_learn: HTML form elements — understanding the different types of form inputs (input, select, textarea, checkbox, radio), attributes (name, id, type, placeholder, required, pattern), and accessibility features
Form validation techniques — learning about client-side validation using JavaScript libraries like Joi, Yup, or Formik, as well as server-side validation using Node.js frameworks like Express.js
HTML5 form validation API — understanding how to use the constraint validation API to validate form inputs without JavaScript
Accessibility guidelines for forms — following best practices for making forms accessible to users with disabilities, including ARIA attributes and label elements
Form submission handling — learning about different ways to handle form submissions, such as using fetch or Axios to send data to a server
State management for forms — understanding how to manage form state using libraries like Redux-form or React Hook Form
Error handling and display — learning about how to display validation errors in a user-friendly way, including using toast notifications or alert messages

knowledge.what_it_is: Ensuring that web applications are accessible to people with disabilities, following Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) 2.1 and Section 508 standards. This includes making content perceivable, operable, understandable, and robust.
what_you_will_learn: Semantic HTML — using HTML elements correctly to provide a clear structure for screen readers
ARIA attributes — adding ARIA roles, states, and properties to enhance accessibility of dynamic content
WCAG guidelines — understanding the principles and techniques for making web content accessible (perceivable, operable, understandable, robust)
Keyboard navigation — ensuring that all interactive elements can be accessed using a keyboard only
Screen reader support — testing with popular screen readers like JAWS, NVDA, VoiceOver to ensure compatibility
Color contrast ratio — meeting the minimum color contrast ratio of 4.5:1 for normal text and 3:1 for larger text
Image description — providing alternative text for images using alt attributes or aria-describedby
Form accessibility — making forms accessible by labeling form fields, grouping related controls, and indicating errors

knowledge.what_it_is: CSS (Cascading Style Sheets) is a styling language used to control the layout and visual appearance of web pages, written in plain text files. It's a crucial part of frontend development, allowing developers to separate presentation from content.
what_you_will_learn: Selectors — CSS rules that target HTML elements using various selectors (e.g., id, class, tag name)
Properties and values — declaring styles for elements using property-value pairs (e.g., color: #fff; font-size: 16px;)
Units and measurements — understanding different units of measurement (e.g., px, em, rem) and how to use them
Box model — visual representation of an element's layout, including margin, border, padding, and content area
CSS specificity — understanding the rules that determine which styles take precedence when multiple selectors apply
Inheritance and cascade — learning how CSS styles are inherited from parent elements and overridden by child elements

knowledge.what_it_is: CSS layout techniques and tools used to create visually appealing and functional user interfaces, including grid systems, flexbox, positioning, and responsive design.
what_you_will_learn: Flexbox — a one-dimensional layout method for aligning items in the main-axis and cross-axis (flex-direction, justify-content, align-items)
Grid System — a two-dimensional layout method for creating complex layouts with rows and columns (grid-template-areas, grid-template-columns, grid-template-rows)
Positioning — absolute, relative, fixed positioning methods for controlling element placement (top, right, bottom, left, z-index)
CSS Grid vs Flexbox — choosing the best layout method for your project
Responsive Design — making layouts adapt to different screen sizes and devices using media queries and percentage-based units
CSS Custom Properties — defining custom variables for colors, fonts, and spacing (var(), calc())
CSS Preprocessors like Sass or Less — writing CSS with variables, functions, and nesting
Layout Utilities in CSS-in-JS libraries like Tailwind CSS or Emotion.js

knowledge.what_it_is: Responsive design is an approach to building user interfaces that adapt to different screen sizes, orientations, and devices. It ensures a smooth user experience across various platforms and devices.
what_you_will_learn: Media Queries — using CSS media queries to apply styles based on screen size, orientation, or device type
Flexible Box Layout (Flexbox) — using the flexbox layout model for flexible and responsive layouts
Grid System — implementing a grid system using CSS Grid or Flexbox for structured and responsive layouts
Responsive Images — using techniques like srcset and sizes to serve images optimized for different screen sizes
Mobile-First Design — designing for mobile devices first, then scaling up to larger screens
Breakpoints — defining specific screen size thresholds (breakpoints) to apply styles and layout changes
Relative Units — using relative units like em, rem, or vw for responsive typography and layouts

knowledge.what_it_is: JavaScript (JS) is a high-level, dynamic, and interpreted programming language used for client-side scripting on the web. It's primarily used for creating interactive web pages, web applications, and mobile applications. As a frontend developer, you'll use JavaScript to create dynamic user interfaces, handle events, and update the Document Object Model (DOM).
what_you_will_learn: Variables and data types — understanding let, const, var, number, string, boolean, null, undefined, object, array
Operators and expressions — learning arithmetic, assignment, comparison, logical, bitwise, and unary operators
Control structures — mastering if/else statements, switch statements, loops (for, while, do-while), and conditional statements
Functions — creating reusable blocks of code with arguments, return types, and function expressions
Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) concepts — understanding classes, inheritance, polymorphism, encapsulation, and prototypal inheritance
DOM manipulation — learning how to select, create, update, and remove elements using methods like querySelector(), createElement(), and appendChild()
Event handling — attaching event listeners to elements for mouse, keyboard, and touch events
Async programming — understanding callbacks, promises, async/await syntax, and error handling with try/catch blocks
Regular expressions (regex) — learning patterns, matching, replacing, and validating strings using regex literals and methods like test(), match(), and replace()
Closures and scope — mastering how to create closures, understand variable hoisting, and manage scope with let, const, and var

knowledge.what_it_is: The Fetch API and XHR are web APIs used to make asynchronous HTTP requests from JavaScript, allowing frontend developers to interact with servers and retrieve or send data. The Fetch API is a modern replacement for XHR and provides a more intuitive and powerful way of making requests.
what_you_will_learn: Making GET requests — sending HTTP requests to the server using the fetch() function
Handling responses — parsing JSON, XML, or other response types using the Response object
Error handling — catching and handling errors using try-catch blocks and the catch() method
POST requests — sending data to the server using the fetch() function with the POST method
Headers and query parameters — setting custom headers and query parameters for requests
CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) — understanding how to make requests from a different origin
AbortController — canceling pending requests using the AbortController API
fetch options — configuring request settings such as mode, credentials, and cache

knowledge.what_it_is: DOM manipulation refers to modifying and updating the Document Object Model (DOM) of an HTML document, which represents the structure and content of a web page. This is crucial for frontend developers as it allows them to dynamically update the UI based on user interactions.
what_you_will_learn: NodeList — accessing and manipulating collections of nodes in the DOM using NodeList API
Element — working with individual HTML elements, including getting and setting attributes, styles, and content
DocumentFragment — creating a lightweight container for multiple nodes to improve rendering performance
appendChild() / insertBefore() — adding new nodes to the DOM while preserving existing node order
createElement() / createTextNode() — dynamically creating new elements and text nodes
querySelector() / querySelectorAll() — selecting specific elements using CSS selectors
getElementsByClassName() / getElementsByTagName() — retrieving collections of elements by class or tag name
innerHTML / outerHTML — accessing and modifying the HTML content of an element
subtopics: event delegation using event.target and event.currenttarget
mutation observers for detecting dom changes
virtual dom libraries (e.g. react, angular) and their implications on dom manipulation

knowledge.what_it_is: Version control systems (VCS) are essential tools for frontend developers to manage code changes, collaborate with teams, and maintain a record of project history. Git is the most widely used VCS in the industry.
what_you_will_learn: Git basics — understanding commit, branch, merge, push, pull, fetch, and stash commands
Git workflow — learning to use feature branches, pull requests, and code reviews for collaborative development
GitHub/GitLab integration — setting up remote repositories, managing access permissions, and tracking project activity
Version control best practices — understanding the importance of commit messages, branch naming conventions, and regular backups
Merge conflicts resolution — learning to resolve conflicts using Git's merge tools and strategies for avoiding them
Git hooks — customizing Git behavior with pre-commit, post-commit, and other hooks for automated tasks
Git submodules — managing dependencies and third-party code within a project repository
Git LFS (Large File Storage) — tracking and versioning large binary files like images and videos
SVN/Git migration strategies — understanding how to migrate from SVN to Git or vice versa
VCS hosting platforms — comparing features, pricing, and integrations of GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket, and Azure DevOps

knowledge.what_it_is: Git is a free and open-source distributed version control system designed to handle small to very large projects with speed and efficiency. It is widely used by frontend developers to manage code changes, collaborate with team members, and track project history.
what_you_will_learn: Repository management — creating, cloning, and managing local and remote repositories
Branching and merging — understanding the concept of branches, creating new branches, merging changes, and resolving conflicts
Commit messages — writing clear and concise commit messages following the Conventional Commits format
Git staging area — adding files to the staging area, committing changes, and reverting commits
Remote repositories — pushing and pulling changes to and from remote repositories like GitHub or GitLab
Gitignore files — creating and managing .gitignore files to exclude unnecessary files from version control
Diff and patch commands — using git diff and git apply to understand code differences and apply patches
Rebase vs merge — understanding the difference between rebasing and merging, and when to use each
Squashing commits — combining multiple commits into a single commit for better project history
Git tags — creating and managing tags to mark important milestones in the project history

knowledge.what_it_is: Bitbucket is a web-based source code repository manager hosted by Atlassian, used for version control and collaboration on software development projects.
what_you_will_learn: Creating repositories — setting up new Bitbucket repos for frontend projects, including configuring permissions and access controls
Cloning repositories — using Git to clone Bitbucket repos onto local machines for development and testing
Branch management — understanding how to create, merge, and manage branches in Bitbucket for feature development and deployment
Pull requests — reviewing and merging code changes from other developers through Bitbucket's pull request workflow
Code reviews — participating in code review processes using Bitbucket's built-in review tools and integrations with other Atlassian products
Webhooks and integrations — setting up webhooks to trigger automated workflows and integrating Bitbucket with other development tools like Jenkins and CircleCI

knowledge.what_it_is: GitHub is a web-based platform for version control and collaboration on software development projects, widely used by frontend developers to manage code repositories, track changes, and collaborate with team members.
what_you_will_learn: Repository structure — organizing project files and folders using GitHub's directory structure
Branching and merging — creating branches for feature development, testing, and deployment, and merging changes back into the main branch
Commit messages and history — writing descriptive commit messages, using imperative mood, and understanding commit history with Git log
Pull requests and code reviews — creating pull requests, assigning reviewers, and participating in code review discussions
GitHub Actions — automating workflows for building, testing, and deploying frontend projects using GitHub's CI/CD tool
GitHub Pages — hosting static websites directly from GitHub repositories using GitHub Pages
Collaboration with teams — managing team access, permissions, and roles within a repository, and understanding GitHub's permission system

knowledge.what_it_is: GitLab is a web-based DevOps lifecycle tool that provides source code management, version control using Git, and CI/CD pipelines. It is widely used by frontend developers to manage and deploy their projects.
what_you_will_learn: Creating repositories and managing access controls — understanding how to create, clone, and manage GitLab repositories for frontend projects
Using GitLab CI/CD pipelines — setting up automated builds, tests, and deployments using YAML files and .gitlab-ci.yml
Version control with GitLab — understanding Git concepts like commits, branches, merges, and resolving conflicts in a frontend context
Managing dependencies with npm or yarn — integrating package managers with GitLab CI/CD pipelines for efficient dependency management
Using GitLab's built-in code review tool — understanding how to create, assign, and manage code reviews for frontend projects
Integrating with other tools like Slack, Jira, or Trello — using GitLab's APIs to integrate with popular project management and collaboration tools

knowledge.what_it_is: npm (Node Package Manager) is a package manager for JavaScript, used to manage dependencies and packages in a project. It was created by Isaac Schlueter and is now maintained by npm, Inc.
what_you_will_learn: package.json — the central configuration file for npm, listing dependencies, scripts, and metadata
dependencies vs devDependencies — understanding the difference between production and development dependencies
npm install — installing packages from the registry or a local directory
npm update — updating installed packages to their latest version
npm uninstall — removing packages from the project
npm ls — listing all installed packages, including dependencies
npm dedupe — removing duplicate dependencies across the project
npm audit — scanning for known vulnerabilities in installed packages

knowledge.what_it_is: pnpm (Package Manager Plus) is a fast, disk space efficient package manager for JavaScript projects, designed to replace npm and yarn. It uses a content-addressable storage system, which allows it to store packages in a more compact form, reducing the overall size of the project's node_modules directory.
what_you_will_learn: pnpm install — installing dependencies using pnpm, including support for workspaces
pnpm link — creating symbolic links between projects, enabling sharing of dependencies without duplication
pnpm store — managing package storage and caching, including automatic cleanup of unused packages
pnpm ls — displaying dependency tree and information about installed packages
pnpm publish — publishing packages to npm registry using pnpm's built-in support
pnpm init — initializing a new project with pnpm, including setting up configuration files
monorepos and workspaces — managing multiple projects within a single repository using pnpm's workspace feature

knowledge.what_it_is: Yarn is a popular, open-source package manager for JavaScript projects, created by Facebook and maintained by the OpenJS Foundation. It was designed to improve upon npm's (Node Package Manager) limitations and provide a faster, more reliable way to manage dependencies in frontend projects.
what_you_will_learn: yarn init — initializing a new project with yarn, including creating a package.json file
yarn add — installing packages from the command line, including specifying version numbers or ranges
yarn install — automatically installing all dependencies listed in package.json, including resolving conflicts and installing devDependencies
yarn link — linking local projects as dependencies, allowing for easy development and testing of new libraries
yarn publish — publishing packages to the npm registry, making them available for other developers to use
yarn workspace — managing monorepos with yarn workspaces, enabling multiple packages to share a single package.json file
yarn berry — using the latest version of yarn (2.x) and its new features, including improved performance and security

knowledge.what_it_is: React is a JavaScript library for building user interfaces, developed and maintained by Meta (Facebook). It's component-based, uses a virtual DOM for efficient rendering, and has a rich ecosystem of tools and libraries. As a frontend developer, you'll use React to build reusable UI components, manage state, and handle interactions.
what_you_will_learn: JSX — writing HTML-like syntax in JS, compiled by Babel/SWC
Functional components — the modern standard for building React UIs, replacing class components
Props — passing data down the component tree (read-only in child components)
State management with useState — local component state management, triggers re-render when updated
useEffect — handling side effects: data fetching, subscriptions, DOM manipulation after render
Context API — sharing global state across the component tree without external state management libraries
Custom hooks — extracting reusable stateful logic into standalone functions (useLocalStorage, useFetch, useDebounce)
React.memo — preventing unnecessary re-renders of functional components via shallow prop comparison
Code splitting with React.lazy() and Suspense for dynamic imports and better bundle loading performance
Error Boundaries — catching and handling JavaScript errors in the component tree gracefully (class component requirement)
Reconciliation and virtual DOM diffing — understanding how React decides what to update in the real DOM using its fiber architecture
useContext — consuming React Context without prop drilling through intermediate components
useReducer — managing complex state logic with a reducer pattern similar to Redux
useMemo — memoizing expensive computed values to avoid unnecessary recalculation on re-render
useCallback — memoizing callback functions to prevent unnecessary child component re-renders
useRef — accessing DOM nodes directly and persisting mutable values across renders without triggering re-render
Fragment — a lightweight way to group components without adding extra DOM nodes
Portal — rendering children into the document body or another container
Higher-Order Components (HOCs) — wrapping components with additional props or functionality
Render Props — passing a function as a prop to render dynamic content
React Router — client-side routing for single-page applications
Server-Side Rendering (SSR) with Next.js and React Static Site Generators
Type Checking with TypeScript and Flow

knowledge.what_it_is: Vue.js is an open-source, progressive JavaScript framework for building user interfaces and single-page applications (SPAs). It was created by Evan You and is widely used in production environments due to its flexibility, performance, and ease of use. Vue.js is ideal for complex, data-driven UIs and powers major applications like Alibaba, GitLab, and Baidu.
what_you_will_learn: Templates — declarative syntax for rendering HTML-like structures using interpolation, directives, and expressions
Components — reusable UI building blocks with their own templates, props, and lifecycle hooks
Props — passing data down the component tree (read-only in child components) using v-bind shorthand
State Management — using Vuex for centralized state management, store creation, and action dispatching
Lifecycle Hooks — methods called at different stages of a component's life cycle (created, mounted, updated, destroyed)
Computed Properties — automatic recalculation of values based on dependencies using getters and setters
Watchers — observing property changes and executing code in response to updates
Mixins — reusable functions for injecting common functionality into components
Slots — rendering child components or custom content within a parent component's template
v-if, v-else, v-show directives — conditional rendering based on boolean expressions or dynamic values
v-for loop directive — iterating over arrays or objects to render lists of items
v-on shorthand for event handling — binding methods to events using the @ symbol
v-bind shorthand for attribute binding — setting attributes dynamically using the : symbol
v-model two-way data binding — synchronizing input values with component state
Scopes and Contexts — understanding how Vue.js manages scope and context within components
Render Functions — custom rendering logic using a function-based API
Async Components — lazy loading of components using the <Suspense> component
Server-Side Rendering (SSR) — pre-rendering content on the server for SEO and performance benefits

knowledge.what_it_is: Angular is an open-source, TypeScript-based web application framework developed by Google. It's designed to help developers build complex, scalable, and maintainable applications using a component-based architecture.
what_you_will_learn: Components — building blocks of Angular apps, encapsulating presentation logic and template
Templates — HTML-like syntax for describing UI structure, using interpolation, binding, and directives
Dependency Injection (DI) — managing dependencies between components using the @Injectable decorator
Services — reusable classes providing business logic, data access, or utility functions
Modules — organizing related components, services, and features into a cohesive unit
Routing — client-side navigation between views, using the Angular Router API
Forms — handling user input, validation, and submission using the Angular Forms API
Observables — working with asynchronous data streams using RxJS operators and Observables
HttpClient — making HTTP requests to APIs, using the Angular HttpClient service
Pipes — transforming data in templates using custom pipe functions
Directives — extending HTML elements with custom behavior, using attribute or structural directives
Lifecycle Hooks — executing code at different stages of a component's life cycle (ngAfterViewInit)
Change Detection — understanding how Angular detects changes to component properties and updates the view
Dependency Tree — visualizing dependencies between components and services for debugging and optimization
Angular CLI — using the official command-line interface for creating, building, and serving Angular projects
TypeScript Configuration — configuring TypeScript settings for an Angular project (tsconfig.json)
Angular Modules — understanding the different types of modules in Angular (NgModule, FeatureModule)
Feature Flags — conditionalizing feature implementation using feature flags and environment-specific configurations
AOT Compilation — compiling components at build time using Ahead-of-Time compilation
Tree Shaking — removing unused code from bundles using tree shaking techniques
Code Splitting — dynamically loading modules and features on demand using lazy loading
Error Handling — catching and displaying errors in a user-friendly manner using the Angular ErrorHandler
Security Best Practices — implementing secure coding practices, such as input validation and data encryption

knowledge.what_it_is: Svelte is a lightweight, compiler-based UI framework that allows developers to write efficient and scalable frontend code without the need for complex libraries or frameworks like React. It compiles Svelte components into optimized JavaScript at build time, eliminating the need for runtime DOM manipulation.
what_you_will_learn: Components — building blocks of Svelte applications, similar to React functional components
Template literals — writing HTML-like syntax in JS, compiled by Svelte's compiler
Lifecycle methods — onMount, beforeUpdate, afterUpdate, and onDestroy for handling component lifecycle events
Stores — managing global state across the application using a simple, reactive API
Actions — encapsulating complex logic within reusable functions that can be used throughout the app
Transitions — animating UI changes with a simple, declarative syntax
Slotting — rendering custom content within Svelte components using a slot-based API
Event handling — capturing and emitting events between components using Svelte's event system
Context API — sharing global state across the component tree without external state management libraries
SvelteKit — a framework for building server-side rendered (SSR) and statically generated websites with Svelte
Sapper — a framework for building server-side rendered (SSR) and statically generated applications with Svelte
Preact compatibility — using Preact components within Svelte applications
TypeScript support — writing Svelte components in TypeScript for improved code maintainability and scalability

knowledge.what_it_is: Solid is a modern JavaScript framework developed by Microsoft, focusing on building reusable UI components and leveraging TypeScript for type safety. It's designed to be highly performant, scalable, and maintainable.
what_you_will_learn: Components — defining reusable UI elements with SolidJS, including lifecycle methods and props
State Management — using the @solid-js/store library or built-in store API for managing application state
Effects — handling side effects like data fetching, subscriptions, and DOM manipulation in a declarative way
Computed — memoizing expensive computations to avoid unnecessary recalculations on re-render
Derived — creating derived values from other computed values, useful for caching or debouncing
Suspense — loading data asynchronously while rendering a fallback component, similar to React.lazy()
Router — using the @solid-js/router library for client-side routing and navigation
Server-Side Rendering (SSR) — enabling server-side rendering of SolidJS components with libraries like Next.js or Vite

knowledge.what_it_is: Qwik is a modern, fast, and lightweight JavaScript framework for building web applications, developed by the creators of Angular. It aims to provide a more efficient and scalable alternative to traditional frameworks like React and Vue.js.
what_you_will_learn: Components — building blocks of Qwik apps, similar to React components
Template Literals — using JavaScript strings with embedded expressions for HTML-like syntax
Lifecycle Hooks — custom hooks for managing component lifecycle events (e.g., mount, update, unmount)
Dependency Injection — a design pattern for providing dependencies to components in a decoupled way

knowledge.what_it_is: Astro is a modern, fast, and flexible framework for building static websites and applications, created by the team behind SvelteKit. It's designed to be highly customizable and integrates well with other tools like Vite and Webpack.
what_you_will_learn: Components — Astro's core concept, similar to React or Vue, but with a focus on simplicity and ease of use
Pages — the top-level components in an Astro app, responsible for rendering HTML content
Routes — defining client-side routes using Astro's built-in routing system
Server Components — writing server-side code that can be reused on the client-side using Astro's Server Component API
Islands — a way to render server-side components on the client-side, reducing the need for hydration
Hydration — pre-rendering HTML content on the server and hydrating it with JavaScript on the client-side
Astro Components vs. Svelte Components — understanding the differences between Astro's component model and Svelte's
Integrating with other tools — using Astro with Vite, Webpack, or other build tools to optimize performance

knowledge.what_it_is: Next.js is an open-source, React-based framework for building server-side rendered (SSR) and statically generated websites and applications. It provides a set of features and tools to optimize performance, improve SEO, and enhance developer experience.
what_you_will_learn: Server-Side Rendering (SSR) — rendering React components on the server, improving SEO and page load times
Static Site Generation (SSG) — pre-rendering pages at build time, reducing server load and improving performance
Routing with Next.js API Routes — defining routes using JavaScript files, enabling server-side routing and API integration
Internationalization (i18n) and Localization (L10n) — handling multiple languages and regions in your application
Next.js Image Optimization — automatically optimizing images for web use, reducing file size and improving page load times
GetStaticProps and GetServerSideProps — fetching data on the server or at build time, enabling dynamic content generation
Dynamic Imports with Next.js — loading modules dynamically, reducing initial bundle size and improving performance

knowledge.what_it_is: Nuxt.js is a popular, open-source Vue.js framework for building server-side rendered (SSR) and statically generated websites and applications. It provides a set of tools and features to simplify the development process and improve performance.
what_you_will_learn: Server-Side Rendering (SSR) — rendering Vue components on the server, improving SEO and page load times
Static Site Generation (SSG) — pre-rendering pages at build time for faster page loads and better SEO
Vue Router Integration — seamless integration with Vue Router for client-side routing and navigation
Async Data Loading — loading data asynchronously using Nuxt's built-in fetch API or third-party libraries like Axios
Layouts and Components — creating reusable layouts and components to simplify the development process
Nuxt Modules — extending Nuxt's functionality with custom modules, such as authentication and analytics
Internationalization (i18n) and Localization (L10n) — handling multiple languages and regions using Nuxt's built-in i18n module

knowledge.what_it_is: VuePress is an open-source, Vue-powered static site generator (SSG) built on top of Webpack and Vue.js. It allows developers to create fast, secure, and SEO-friendly websites with minimal configuration.
what_you_will_learn: Configuring VuePress — understanding the basic configuration options, such as theme, layout, and navigation
Writing Markdown content — learning how to write Markdown files for blog posts, documentation, and other types of content
Using Vue components in Markdown — understanding how to embed Vue components within Markdown files using the `@vuepress/component` directive
Customizing layouts and themes — learning how to create custom layouts and themes using VuePress's built-in support for Webpack and Vue.js
Integrating with other tools and services — understanding how to integrate VuePress with other tools and services, such as GitHub Pages, Netlify, and Vercel
Optimizing performance and SEO — learning how to optimize the performance and SEO of a VuePress site using techniques such as code splitting, caching, and meta tags

knowledge.what_it_is: Hugo is an open-source, blazing-fast static site generator written in Go, ideal for building fast and secure websites with a minimal learning curve.
what_you_will_learn: Templates — writing Hugo templates using HTML, CSS, and Go syntax to generate dynamic content
Front Matter — adding metadata to Markdown files using YAML or TOML syntax for customizing layouts and rendering
Shortcodes — creating reusable code snippets in Markdown files for embedding interactive elements like videos or maps
Taxonomies — organizing content using built-in taxonomies like categories, tags, and authors for improved navigation
URLs and Permalinks — configuring custom URLs and permalinks for SEO-friendly and user-friendly links
Image Processing — optimizing images using Hugo's built-in image processing features for faster page loads
Internationalization (i18n) — translating content using Hugo's i18n support for multilingual websites

knowledge.what_it_is: OWASP (Open Web Application Security Project) Security Risks are a list of common web application security vulnerabilities, specifically designed to help frontend developers identify and prevent security issues in their applications. These risks are categorized into several types, including injection, cross-site scripting (XSS), cross-site request forgery (CSRF), and more.
what_you_will_learn: SQL Injection — injecting malicious SQL code into user input fields to extract or modify sensitive data
Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) — injecting client-side scripts into web pages to steal user data, take control of user sessions, or spread malware
Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) — tricking users into performing unintended actions on a web application by manipulating HTTP requests
Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) Prevention — using Content Security Policy (CSP), HTML escaping, and other techniques to prevent XSS attacks
DOM-based XSS — exploiting vulnerabilities in the Document Object Model (DOM) to inject malicious scripts into web pages
Server-side Request Forgery (SSRF) — tricking servers into making unintended requests to external systems or services
JavaScript Injection — injecting malicious JavaScript code into user input fields to extract or modify sensitive data

knowledge.what_it_is: A Content Security Policy (CSP) is a security feature that helps prevent cross-site scripting (XSS) attacks by defining which sources of content are allowed to be executed within a web page. It's a critical aspect of frontend development, ensuring the security and integrity of user data.
what_you_will_learn: Policy directives — declaring allowed sources for scripts, stylesheets, images, fonts, and more
Policy headers — setting HTTP headers to specify policy directives (Content-Security-Policy, X-Frame-Options)
Nonce generation — using a random value in policy headers to prevent cache poisoning attacks
Hash-based policies — specifying allowed sources using cryptographic hashes (e.g., SHA-256)
Report-only policies — logging potential security issues without blocking them
Policy refresh — updating policy directives dynamically based on user interactions or server-side data
CSP reporting API — parsing and processing CSP reports to identify vulnerabilities

knowledge.what_it_is: Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS) is an HTTP header mechanism that allows web servers to relax the same-origin policy, enabling web pages to make requests to a different origin (domain, protocol, or port). This is essential for modern web applications that rely on third-party APIs and services.
what_you_will_learn: Origin headers — understanding how browsers send origin information in HTTP requests
Access-Control-Allow-Origin header — configuring the server to allow cross-origin requests
Preflight requests — understanding the OPTIONS request sent by browsers before making a CORS request
CORS modes — learning about the different CORS modes (simple, actual, and preflight)
CORS credentials — understanding how to handle credentials in CORS requests (e.g., cookies, authorization headers)
CORS proxying — using reverse proxies to bypass CORS restrictions for development or testing purposes
CORS security considerations — learning about potential security risks and best practices for implementing CORS

knowledge.what_it_is: HTTPS (Hypertext Transfer Protocol Secure) is a secure communication protocol used to transfer data between a web browser and a server, ensuring confidentiality, integrity, and authenticity of data. It uses TLS (Transport Layer Security) for encryption and relies on certificates issued by trusted Certificate Authorities.
what_you_will_learn: SSL/TLS handshake — the process of establishing an encrypted connection between client and server
Certificate Authority (CA) — issuing and managing digital certificates, including SSL certificates
Public Key Infrastructure (PKI) — a system for managing public-private key pairs and digital certificates
HTTPS headers — HTTP headers used to negotiate encryption and authentication, such as 'Content-Security-Policy' and 'Strict-Transport-Security'
HTTP/2 over TLS — using the TLS protocol to enable multiplexing and other features of HTTP/2
Certificate Pinning — securely binding a certificate's public key to a specific domain or IP address
Certificate Transparency (CT) — a system for logging and monitoring SSL certificates, ensuring authenticity
TLS 1.3 — the latest version of the TLS protocol, offering improved security features such as zero-round-trip authentication

knowledge.what_it_is: Browser Storage APIs provide a way to store and retrieve data locally on the client-side, allowing web applications to persist data even after page reloads or closures.
what_you_will_learn: Local Storage — storing key-value pairs in the browser's local storage, accessible via `localStorage` API
Session Storage — storing key-value pairs for the duration of a single browsing session, accessible via `sessionStorage` API
IndexedDB — a client-side NoSQL database for storing structured data, accessed via `indexedDB` API
Web Storage API Quotas — understanding storage limits and strategies for optimizing storage usage
Cache API — using the Cache API to store responses from network requests, improving page load performance
Service Workers Caching — leveraging Service Worker caching mechanisms to store resources locally
Storage Events — handling storage events to detect changes in stored data

knowledge.what_it_is: WebSockets is a bi-directional communication protocol that enables real-time data exchange between a web browser and a server over a persistent connection, allowing for efficient and low-latency updates to the client-side application state.
what_you_will_learn: WebSocket API — accessing WebSocket functionality through JavaScript's WebSocket interface
Establishing connections — creating and managing WebSocket connections using the `ws` protocol or alternative libraries like Socket.IO
Sending messages — transmitting data from the server to the client using the `send()` method
Receiving messages — handling incoming messages from the server using event listeners (e.g., `onmessage`)
Closing connections — terminating WebSocket connections using the `close()` method or alternative methods like Socket.IO's `disconnect()`
Error handling — managing errors that occur during WebSocket communication, such as connection timeouts or disconnections
WebSocket events — understanding and handling various events triggered by WebSocket connections (e.g., `open`, `message`, `error`, `close`)
Socket.IO — using a popular library for simplifying WebSocket development and adding features like automatic reconnection and event emitters

knowledge.what_it_is: Server-Sent Events (SSE) is an API that allows servers to push events to connected clients, enabling real-time communication and updates. It's a unidirectional communication channel where the server pushes data to the client without any request from the client.
what_you_will_learn: EventSource — creating an SSE connection using JavaScript, specifying event source URL
addEventListener — attaching event listeners for specific events (e.g., 'message', 'error')
onmessage — handling incoming messages from the server, accessing data attributes and event.data
onerror — catching and handling errors that occur during SSE communication
open — opening the SSE connection, specifying optional headers and event stream ID
close — closing the SSE connection, releasing system resources
reconnecting — automatically reconnecting to the SSE server in case of disconnections

knowledge.what_it_is: A Service Worker is a script that runs between the client and server, enabling offline support, caching, and push notifications for web applications.
what_you_will_learn: Registration — registering a service worker with navigator.serviceWorker.register() and handling registration errors
Installation — installing a service worker with fetch events and handling installation failures
Activation — activating a service worker with activation events and handling activation failures
Cache API — using the Cache API to cache resources, including caching strategies and cache invalidation
Fetch Event — handling fetch events in a service worker to intercept requests and responses
Push Messages — receiving push messages from a server and displaying notifications to the user
Background Sync — synchronizing data between offline and online states using background sync
Service Worker Lifecycle — understanding the different stages of a service worker's lifecycle, including registration, installation, activation, and termination

knowledge.what_it_is: Geolocation APIs allow web applications to access and share location information with users, providing features like map integration, geocoding, and proximity-based services.
what_you_will_learn: Geolocation API — accessing user's current location using navigator.geolocation.getCurrentPosition() or watchPosition(), returning latitude, longitude, accuracy, and timestamp
HTML5 Geolocation — implementing geolocation in web applications using HTML5 features like geolocation events and position updates
Google Maps JavaScript API — integrating Google Maps into frontend applications for map rendering, directions, and geocoding
Leaflet.js — a lightweight JavaScript library for creating interactive maps with various overlays, markers, and popups
Geohash — encoding latitude and longitude coordinates as a string of characters to enable efficient spatial indexing and querying
GeoJSON — representing geographic data in JSON format for easy parsing and manipulation by frontend applications
W3C Geolocation API — a standardized interface for accessing location information on various devices, including desktops, laptops, and mobile phones

knowledge.what_it_is: Notifications are used to inform users of events or updates, and can be implemented using various APIs and libraries in frontend development.
what_you_will_learn: Web Notifications API — a W3C standard for displaying notifications in the browser, including permission requests and notification options
Notification.permission — checking the user's current notification permission status
Notification.requestPermission() — requesting permission to display notifications
Service Workers and Push API — using service workers to handle push notifications and background synchronization
Push API — sending push notifications from a server to the client, including support for web sockets and server-sent events
Notification.permission.queryOption — specifying options for notification queries, such as whether to show notifications while the app is in the background
Notification.close() — closing a notification programmatically
Notification.tag — assigning a tag to a notification for later retrieval or dismissal

knowledge.what_it_is: Device orientation refers to the ability of a web application to detect and respond to changes in device orientation, such as from portrait to landscape mode.
what_you_will_learn: Window.orientation — accessing the current device orientation (0-360 degrees)
Screen.orientation — setting or getting the screen's preferred orientation
Media query: orientation — using CSS media queries to apply styles based on device orientation
DeviceMotion API — detecting and responding to device motion events, including orientation changes
Orientationchange event — handling orientation changes with JavaScript event listeners

knowledge.what_it_is: Payment APIs enable frontend developers to securely process transactions, manage payment methods, and handle sensitive user data. Popular libraries like Stripe.js, PayPal JavaScript SDK, and Braintree.js provide a secure and efficient way to integrate payments into web applications.
what_you_will_learn: Stripe.js — integrating Stripe's payment processing API with frontend code, handling events, and managing payment methods
PayPal JavaScript SDK — using PayPal's client-side library for payment processing, tokenization, and checkout integration
Braintree.js — integrating Braintree's payment gateway with frontend code, handling transactions, and managing payment methods
Payment Request API — enabling secure payments through a standardized interface, supporting multiple payment methods and providers
Web Cryptography API — securely storing and managing sensitive user data, such as credit card numbers and expiration dates
Tokenization — replacing sensitive payment information with tokens for secure storage and transmission
3D Secure 2.0 — implementing additional security measures for online transactions, reducing friction and improving conversion rates

knowledge.what_it_is: Browser credentials are used to authenticate users with web applications, allowing access to protected resources and APIs. The Web Credentials API provides a secure way to store and retrieve user credentials, such as username and password or biometric data.
what_you_will_learn: Credential management — storing and retrieving user credentials securely using the Web Credentials API
Credential creation — creating new credentials for users, including setting expiration dates and permissions
Credential storage — securely storing credentials in the browser's credential store, protected by the operating system
Credential retrieval — retrieving stored credentials using the Web Credentials API, with options to refresh or update credentials
Credential validation — validating user-provided credentials against stored credentials, including support for password hashing and salting
Biometric authentication — integrating biometric data, such as fingerprint or facial recognition, into credential management
FIDO2 authentication — using the Web Authentication API to integrate FIDO2-compliant hardware security keys with web applications

knowledge.what_it_is: The RAIL model is a performance optimization framework developed by Google, used to measure and improve the responsiveness of web applications. It stands for Response, Animation, Idle, Load, and provides guidelines for developers to prioritize tasks and optimize their code.
what_you_will_learn: Response — understanding how users perceive latency and optimizing critical path tasks
Animation — learning about animation best practices, including frame rate, duration, and easing functions
Idle — identifying opportunities to improve user experience during idle periods, such as prefetching or caching
Load — optimizing the loading of assets, including images, scripts, and stylesheets
Prioritization — applying RAIL principles to prioritize tasks and optimize code for performance
Web Vitals — understanding how to measure and track web vitals, including Largest Contentful Paint (LCP), First Input Delay (FID), and Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS)
Performance Budgeting — setting and enforcing performance budgets based on RAIL guidelines

knowledge.what_it_is: Performance metrics are essential for frontend developers to measure and optimize the speed, responsiveness, and overall user experience of web applications. This includes metrics such as page load time, time to first paint, and time to interactive.
what_you_will_learn: Page Load Time (PLT) — measuring the time it takes for a webpage to fully load, including all assets and content
Time To First Paint (TTFP) — measuring the time it takes for the first pixel of a webpage to be rendered
Time To Interactive (TTI) — measuring the time it takes for a webpage to become interactive, allowing users to start interacting with it
First Contentful Paint (FCP) — measuring the time it takes for the first piece of content on a webpage to be rendered
Speed Index (SI) — measuring the average time it takes for a webpage's content to be visible to the user
Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS) — measuring the visual stability of a webpage, with lower scores indicating less layout shift
Web Vitals API — using the Web Vitals API to measure and report on performance metrics in modern browsers
Lighthouse Performance Metrics — understanding how Lighthouse measures and reports on performance metrics such as PLT, TTFP, and TTI

knowledge.what_it_is: Lighthouse is an open-source, automated tool for auditing and improving web application performance, accessibility, and best practices. It provides a comprehensive report on various aspects of a website's quality, including performance, SEO, accessibility, and more.
what_you_will_learn: Configuring Lighthouse audits — understanding the different types of audits (performance, accessibility, SEO) and how to customize them
Running Lighthouse from the command line — using CLI flags to analyze specific pages or URLs
Analyzing Lighthouse reports — interpreting performance metrics (FCP, FID, CLS), accessibility issues, and SEO recommendations
Using Lighthouse CI/CD integration — automating audits in continuous integration pipelines for real-time feedback
Lighthouse's PWA audit — understanding the requirements and best practices for Progressive Web Apps
Accessibility features in Lighthouse — identifying and fixing common accessibility issues (A11y) using the tool's built-in checks

knowledge.what_it_is: DevTools is a set of web developer tools built into Google Chrome and other Chromium-based browsers, providing a powerful way to inspect, debug, and optimize frontend code. It's an essential tool for any frontend developer.
what_you_will_learn: Inspecting the DOM — using the Elements panel to explore and manipulate HTML elements
Debugging JavaScript — using the Sources panel to set breakpoints, step through code, and inspect variables
Using the Console — understanding how to use the console to log messages, errors, and warnings
Profiling performance — using the Performance panel to identify bottlenecks and optimize frontend code
Analyzing memory usage — using the Memory panel to detect memory leaks and optimize resource allocation
Inspecting network requests — using the Network panel to analyze request headers, response bodies, and timing information

knowledge.what_it_is: The PRPL pattern is a performance optimization technique used in frontend development to prioritize and load critical resources, ensuring a smooth user experience. It stands for Prioritize, Render, Preload, and Lazy-load.
what_you_will_learn: Prioritization — identifying and loading the most essential resources first, using techniques like code splitting and dynamic imports
Rendering — rendering the initial UI with critical components, using libraries like React or Vue.js
Preloading — preloading non-essential resources in the background, using tools like Workbox or PWA-Kit
Lazy-loading — loading non-critical resources on demand, using techniques like code splitting and dynamic imports
Resource optimization — optimizing images, fonts, and other assets for faster loading times
Caching strategies — implementing caching mechanisms to reduce the number of requests made to the server

knowledge.what_it_is: Performance best practices are essential for frontend developers to ensure that web applications load quickly, respond rapidly, and provide a seamless user experience. This includes optimizing code, reducing latency, and leveraging browser features to improve performance.
what_you_will_learn: Code splitting — breaking down large codebases into smaller chunks to reduce initial payload size and improve page loading times
Lazy loading — deferring non-essential resources until they are needed, reducing initial load time and improving user experience
Tree shaking — removing unused code from bundles to reduce file size and improve performance
Minification and compression — reducing code size through techniques like Gzip and Brotli compression
Caching — storing frequently accessed data in memory or local storage to reduce latency and improve responsiveness
Image optimization — compressing images using tools like ImageOptim or ShortPixel to reduce file size and improve page loading times
Font optimization — optimizing font sizes, styles, and formats to reduce file size and improve performance
Avoiding unnecessary DOM reflows and repaints — minimizing the number of changes to the DOM to reduce rendering overhead

knowledge.what_it_is: A Web App Manifest is an essential file that provides metadata about a web application, enabling features like progressive web apps (PWAs), offline support, and home screen installation. It's a JSON file that contains information about the app's name, icons, themes, and other settings.
what_you_will_learn: Manifest file structure — understanding the required fields and their usage in the manifest.json file
Short name and display name — setting the short name for the home screen and display name for the title bar
Icons — adding multiple icons with different sizes and formats, including PNG, ICO, and SVG
Splash screens — customizing the splash screen that appears when the app is launched from a home screen or bookmark
Themes — defining themes for the app, including colors, backgrounds, and fonts
Start URL — specifying the URL where the app should start when launched
Background modes — enabling background tasks like geolocation, push notifications, and camera access
Scope — setting the scope of the app's permissions and access to specific domains or URLs
subtopics: background modes geolocation push notifications camera access
validation_keywords: background modes geolocation push notifications camera access

knowledge.what_it_is: The Cache API provides a low-level interface to manage cache storage, enabling web applications to store and retrieve data locally or remotely. It allows developers to create custom caching strategies, improve performance, and reduce network requests.
what_you_will_learn: CacheStorage — accessing the browser's cache storage using JavaScript
Request Cache API — controlling cache behavior for specific requests using the Cache API
Response Cache API — enabling servers to specify cache headers and control caching behavior
Cache-Control header — understanding how to use HTTP headers to manage caching
ETag and Last-Modified headers — using these headers to implement cache invalidation strategies
Service Workers and Caching — leveraging Service Workers to cache resources and improve offline support
Cache API and Web Storage — comparing and contrasting the two for storing data locally

knowledge.what_it_is: Offline support patterns enable web applications to function seamlessly even when users are disconnected from the internet, ensuring a smooth user experience and improving overall application reliability.
what_you_will_learn: Service Workers — registering scripts that run in the background, intercepting network requests, and caching resources for offline access
Cache API — accessing cached resources, storing data locally, and implementing cache invalidation strategies
Offline Storage — using IndexedDB or WebSQL to store data locally, ensuring offline functionality even without a network connection
PWA Manifest — configuring the web app manifest to specify offline support features, such as caching and storage requirements
Cache-Control Headers — understanding how to configure HTTP headers for cache control, including max-age, immutable, and no-cache directives
Service Worker Lifecycle — managing service worker registration, activation, and updates, ensuring seamless transitions between online and offline modes

knowledge.what_it_is: Electron is an open-source framework for building cross-platform desktop applications using web technologies such as HTML, CSS, and JavaScript. It allows developers to create native-like apps on Windows, macOS, and Linux with a single codebase.
what_you_will_learn: Creating Electron apps from scratch — setting up the project structure, installing dependencies, and building the initial app
Using Electron's main process vs renderer process — understanding the difference between these two processes and how to communicate between them
Building UI components with HTML, CSS, and JavaScript — using Electron's built-in UI tools such as BrowserWindow, Menu, and Tray
Handling user input and events — using Electron's API for handling keyboard shortcuts, mouse events, and other user interactions
Using Electron's IPC (Inter-Process Communication) mechanism — sending and receiving messages between the main process and renderer processes
Implementing authentication and authorization in Electron apps — using libraries such as Electron-FSWatcher and electron-store
Optimizing performance and memory usage in Electron apps — using techniques such as lazy loading, caching, and minimizing DOM mutations

knowledge.what_it_is: Tauri is a framework for building desktop applications using web technologies, allowing developers to leverage their existing frontend skills and tools to create native-like experiences on Windows, macOS, and Linux.
what_you_will_learn: Building desktop applications with HTML, CSS, and JavaScript — leveraging web technologies to create native-like experiences
Using Rust as a backend language for building Tauri applications, interacting with the OS and hardware through APIs
Tauri's plugin architecture — extending application functionality with custom plugins written in Rust or JavaScript
Handling platform-specific features like drag-and-drop, notifications, and clipboard access using Tauri's API
Integrating with native libraries and frameworks like Electron, Flutter, and Qt using Tauri's foreign function interface (FFI)
Using WebAssembly (WASM) to compile web code for desktop applications, enabling seamless integration of web technologies
Managing application state and data storage using Tauri's built-in support for IndexedDB and other web storage APIs

knowledge.what_it_is: Flutter is an open-source, cross-platform UI development framework created by Google, used to build natively compiled applications for mobile, web, and desktop from a single codebase. It uses the Dart programming language and provides a rich set of tools and libraries for building high-performance, visually appealing user interfaces.
what_you_will_learn: Widgets — building blocks of Flutter UIs, used to create custom layouts and designs
Material Design and Cupertino widgets — pre-built, customizable UI components for Android and iOS respectively
Stateful vs Stateless widgets — understanding the difference between mutable and immutable widget states
InheritedWidget and Provider libraries — managing global state across the app using a provider pattern
Navigator and Router libraries — handling navigation between screens in Flutter apps
Scaffold and AppBar widgets — building custom top-level layouts with toolbars, menus, and other UI elements
GridView and ListView widgets — displaying lists of items with customizable layout and scrolling behavior

knowledge.what_it_is: Ionic is an open-source, cross-platform UI component library for building hybrid mobile apps using web technologies like HTML, CSS, and JavaScript. It provides a set of pre-built components, tools, and services to speed up development and deployment.
what_you_will_learn: Components — Ionic's core building blocks, including cards, buttons, inputs, lists, and more
Pages — structuring app content with navigation, routing, and layout management
Services — accessing native device features like camera, contacts, and geolocation
Directives — customizing component behavior with attributes like ngIf, ngFor, and ngSwitch
Ionic Framework Architecture — understanding the framework's modular design and dependency injection
Angular Integration — using Ionic with Angular for a full-fledged web app development experience
Styling — customizing components with CSS variables, theming, and overrides
Ionic CLI — automating tasks like building, testing, and deploying apps

knowledge.what_it_is: Flutter is an open-source, cross-platform UI framework developed by Google, allowing developers to build natively compiled applications for mobile, web, and desktop from a single codebase. It uses the Dart programming language and provides a rich set of widgets and tools for building complex user interfaces.
what_you_will_learn: Widgets — building blocks of Flutter UIs, including Material Design and Cupertino widgets
Stateful vs Stateless Widgets — understanding when to use each type of widget for dynamic content
InheritedWidget — sharing data between widgets in the tree without using a provider library
Navigator 2.0 — managing app navigation with a new, more flexible API
Provider Library — managing global state and dependencies with a popular third-party package
Riverpod — an alternative to Provider for state management with a more declarative approach
BLoC Pattern — using Business Logic Components to separate presentation logic from business logic
Dart Language Fundamentals — understanding the basics of Dart, including null safety and async/await

knowledge.what_it_is: React Native is an open-source framework developed by Facebook (now Meta) that allows building native mobile applications for Android and iOS using React, a JavaScript library.
what_you_will_learn: Components — building blocks of React Native apps, similar to React components
JSX — writing HTML-like syntax in JS, compiled by Babel/SWC, with additional support for native UI components
Props — passing data down the component tree (read-only in child components), including support for native modules and platform-specific props
State — managing local component state using useState hook or class components, with synchronization across native views
Lifecycle methods — handling app lifecycle events, such as componentDidMount and componentWillUnmount, on both Android and iOS
Native Modules — integrating native code into React Native apps using JavaScript interfaces
Platform-specific APIs — accessing platform-specific features, such as camera, GPS, and contacts, using React Native's bridge
React Navigation — a popular navigation library for React Native, providing declarative routing and navigation between screens
